speaker: interviewer
text: What is this floor made out of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is marble.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's marble?

speaker: interviewee
text: The technique of this is called Palladiana. I can tell you a story about that-- Palladiana. When I planned that room, my wife asked me, what kind of a floor are we going to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Slate. Continuing with Dr. Herbert Zipper, talking to Sandy Jacobson in Los Angeles, California, in English. It's the 9th of February, 1995. Tape number 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So you came back to Vienna. Were you in Vienna--

speaker: interviewee
text: On January-- on January 30, 1933, Hitler came to power, and that was-- that was, for me, my end for Germany. Of course, I stayed until June, because I couldn't leave my students at the conservatory before the end of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, living in Dusseldorf with Hitler in power, as a Jew? Were you looking over your shoulder every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: More than that. Twice I left by night. I was warned. I had quite a number of nice friends there who were Nazis, but who knew very well, and who didn't want me to get into trouble. So once I left for Westphalia, and the other time I left for Holland, just for a few days. Until the so-called air was clear. No, I saw-- I saw the most terrible things. I mean, not-- of course, a friend of mine was shot immediately who was an editor of a newspaper. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Yeah. The worst thing-- one of the worst sights of my life-- the day was May 10th when the-- the book burning started. That was a terrible experience. And when you see this, then you know this is really the greatest danger that we are facing. Because when they start-- when they start burning books, they soon will start burning people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Dusseldorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Dusseldorf until June-- until the school was over-- school year was over, in June, and went to Vienna. And there I was immediately grabbed by some of my old schoolmates and some friends, that it started-- had started, in Vienna, those subterranean political cabarets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cabarets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cabaret. That's where I started working as composer-- mainly as composer. And also, sometimes, played the piano as pianist. And there was a great deal of my life, for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for composing music for the political arena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider yourself, at that time, politically active?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's the first time when I became politically active. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I did also-- I was a bit active in Germany, too. But there I was a foreigner. And it was difficult to identify yourself with the-- the Germans. Always a big difficulty. I wrote to my mother-- I wrote to my mother, once-- uh-- working in Germany is heaven. Living in Germany's hell. It was at that time-- it is now, of course, entirely different. But at that time, living there, for-- for a foreign Jew, was not a very simple thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in '33-- June of '33, you returned to Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- and you worked in the cabarets.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. There were quite a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: So between '33 and '38, you just kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was-- I was in Russia for that time. I did some guest conducting. I was in Russia, I was in Italy, I went to-- I was in England. Summer '36, I was conducting in the Covent Garden, the Russian ballet. And, well, we did all kinds of-- of-- I didn't give up my work entirely. But as a composer, I really worked practically [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all the time. Whatever duties, my-- my pencil was for the-- for the cabaret.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you would go to England, did you ever think, maybe I'd better stay here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a question of-- of family loyalties. I didn't want to stay in England. I wanted to stay in Russia. And I would have been in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I got a contract to stay-- to take over the [NON-ENGLISH], so it was a big city south of Moscow, not far from Moscow. They had a new radio station and a new orchestra. And I would have-- I would have-- I was engaged as the director of the orchestra. And I went just in '36 for Christmas vacation to Vienna and to pick up Trudl and marry her.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then this-- immediately after that, the-- the Stalin-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Stalin problems started. When people were killed, when people-- so they changed the contract. They wanted me to go to Khabarovsk. You know where Khabarovsk is? In east Siberia. Of course, you see, you tell anybody in Vienna you're going to east Siberia, they-- are you going to be in exile for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, it wouldn't have been so bad. Because Khabarovsk is a huge city, with a beautiful opera house. You know where the Russians go, they go to the opera house, there is something going on. [COUGHS] Artistically, always something first-rate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, no, I rejected it, that contract. And there was-- I mean, to think that I would go away from my family for-- for everything that actually-- I mean, I was a European. To go to eastern Siberia, it was unthinkable. Today I would think differently. So then now comes the Anschluss. Can you tell me about that-- how it felt to be this--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, my problem was this. That I saw it. I lived it. And although my whole family knew it, but knowledge and knowing are two different things. Knowledge you can learn from books, and from lectures, and from pictures, and from whatnot. But knowledge means you have lived it. That means both your intellectual and your emotional person has both involved. When that emotional thing is not-- has not been experienced, then knowledge doesn't help you. Knowing doesn't help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a difference between my people. They all didn't live what I lived in Germany. They didn't experience what this was, really. Oh, it won't be that bad. You know that's-- to-- I wanted to leave the same night. You wanted to leave Vienna the night of the election?

speaker: interviewee
text: The night. And my people voted me down, unanimously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family. Except my father, who was in England at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he there?

speaker: interviewee
text: On business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was just outvoted, and I wouldn't leave my-- my family alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I think that I neglected to ask you to say, besides your mother and father-- tell me about you had-- your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was my sister, and her husband, and her two children. There was my brother Walter, and there was my brother Otto who was here at this BMW Zipper. So, I mean, there were-- And this was a pretty sizable family. And I couldn't just leave them alone. Besides, both my brothers were not made to go around and get the papers, going into pay their taxes and whatnot. And we need all these things. I could handle it. I knew I could handle it. And they were relying on me. And my father-- my father, who called up the same night when it happened-- said I'm going immediately. I'm going-- I will be there tomorrow. So I said, Father, if you come back, I will never talk to you again. He understood fully. I didn't know whether the-- the-- the telephone was already bugged or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you suspected that could be--

speaker: interviewee
text: I suspected. I wanted to be careful. I said, Father, if you come back, I will never, never talk  to you again. And he understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the message you were sending him?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the message.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you might not be able to speak to him again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the message was that-- don't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Because if you come back, I may not be able to speak to you again. Something may happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody-- I know what would have happened to him. I know exactly what would have happened to him. No, he immediately thought-- and-- and I was very emphatic about it. I mean, in my voice, he knew that-- that's what he told me later on-- that he knew that I knew a little bit more about the situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were others who-- for instance, when Schuschnigg went on February-- was it February 12 or 13? I don't know. Just a month before the Anschluss, when Schuschnigg-- [COUGHS] excuse me-- went to Berchtesgaden to see Hitler, I knew this-- [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: --I seen that this-- that's end. The father of my-- of one of my-- of my closest friend, Eric Simon, Ernst Simon, who was-- who was a very wealthy businessman, called me up and said, Herbert, how do you see the situation? And I told him, Ernst Simon, if I would be you, I would pack up immediately and leave the country for good. That's what he did. He had a month's time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he left, and he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He left four or five days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. But you couldn't get your own family to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- so now it's-- it's a different Vienna, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it has changed--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I meant then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then, after the Anschluss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Overnight! Overnight! We have no conception of what can happen, if a city that was, for nearly 2,000 years, the capital of a country-- small, larger, very large, then smaller, but still the center of-- that created its own culture, with a certain [NON-ENGLISH] of people that create-- that created and kept this culture. The people like Richard Strauss, with his operas, couldn't be successful in a city where there was not a huge population that supported it. It is-- it is not just the creation of artists, it's a creation that they need to address themselves to people who try to understand. What happened to Vienna overnight was that it suddenly and immediately became a German provincial city, which it never was before, in which the hub of the city-- those who kept its culture-- were suddenly a bunch of outlaws. That is not an exaggeration. Those 180,000 Jews of Vienna became, overnight, complete outlaws. They could be robbed. They could be mishandled. They could be killed-- as they would. They couldn't do-- anything could happen to them. They had no recourse to any legal authority, to the police, to even a lawyer-- to nothing. They were just exposed to a complete bunch of ruffians who took charge. I see the next morning, you have the son of our concierge, down there-- the housekeeper, what we call it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean like a-- in the apartment building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah!

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came up, the son, and said to my brother, the key! My brother said, what, the key? He said, the key of your car! It's mine now. And my brother says, what? And then he made like this, and we saw that he had a-- he had  a gun here. And my brother gave him the key. That was it. And people could-- could stop you on the street, kill you, then. Everybody would look-- look away from it. So, I mean-- And now we must not forget that 80%, let's say, of the law profession was in Jewish hands. There were 186 full-time journalists in Vienna, at the various newspapers, which is a large number, of which 176 were Jewish. So, I mean, the Jewish penetration of the culture, of the intellectual life, of the-- of the professional life, was so strong that that also created, of course, that anti-Semitism about those who did not achieve that. See, what anti-Semitism creates is that, since Jews were not allowed to apply to acquire property for centuries, they had to find another way to stay alive. The only way just to live by their wits. So to sharpen their wits was a thing that, in babyhood, you had to start. That's why they could be good as mathematicians, as writers, as what-- any kind-- which-- which you could take with yourself is Semite. And that's the way how then-- how those, all those people who could get out, lived. I lived-- I came to Paris with $2.50 in my pocket. Everything was taken away from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back-- that's later in the story. When-- when you-- what happened to your friends? You were active in Vienna all your life. Did people shun you, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The friends? I mean, those who were non-Jewish, they wouldn't dare to come. They wouldn't dare to come to see us. In fact, during the following two months, I hardly ever slept at home, because I was-- I was suspect-- I mean, not because I was Jewish only, because I was a radical, on the other side. And, as such I was suspect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't-- I always-- when I went home, I called up my mother if the air is free. She Said, no, don't come. That means there was some-- something going on which-- So I knew-- and that's-- the only one that-- the only time that I was home overnight was just stupid. I was nabbed, of course. The one-- OK. So what happened that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: That night, I was taken to the police, and from the police to another police station. And then I was taken by the-- [COUGHS].

speaker: interviewer
text: You were taken with your brother, weren't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, taken to my-- with my brother. Went down to a-- to a school that was by the Nazis emptied for the prison. There were a few-- a few hundred-- quite a few hundred people there. And then we were shipped to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how it happened that you-- you were taken with your two brothers. But one brother was-- Otto was sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- see, the-- the people who watched us-- that means who-- this was not German police yet. They were Austrian police. And I was still an Austrian, you know? And I went to that policeman and said, you know, that my brother cannot stay here. He has to go to hospital, right away. Oh, he came to-- I said, listen, if he dies here-- and he might-- he might die here, in the next two days-- then I make you responsible. Then you are-- Or, better still, [LAUGHS] Austrian-- it was just funny-- I mean, it was my last act of-- of Austrian superiority. And they let him go-- let him go-- or, no, they put him to a hospital. They found out. That's the way he got free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was saved, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you and Walter, then, were put on a train to go. Now tell me about that train ride.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't want to talk about that again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me briefly about how--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was most beastly, most terrifying, most ugly, most inhuman, most ghastly experience of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ride itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ride itself. Those 13 hours from Vienna to Dachau were the worst thing that I can experience. I mean, there were learned-- Again, I said, after some hours I began to learn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the shock-- the shock went off, I start to learn. And I learned how beastly people can be made-- to what extent they can be manipulated to become worse than animals. I shouldn't even say as animals. Animals are probably very kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Germans, or Austrians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were then Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you feel that the Germans themselves were manipulated-- these soldiers who took you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were manipulated the same way I explained to you-- by-- by the hatred, by-- by-- the obvious thing, you see, Hitler was very clever, in one way, to have the obvious as the thing itself it means. Because there were Jewish bankers, Jewish people who owned department stores. To tell them that's the way they rob the people. And then obvious things to tell them-- that all their ills, all their difficulties, all their problems-- this was this-- what I call the middle-- middle slime of people. I mean, who were half-educated-- or, let's say, a third educated, who didn't know-- really had not the ability to think. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the easy solutions. We have some people here in Congress, who are very, very similar.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- you talked about-- you wrote that the-- that the German soldiers that were transporting you-- they were-- they were-- they were-- their behavior was structured.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, entirely. But they were not soldiers. They were SS people, that were the-- they were the Stormtroopers. They were the Hitler guard. They were the people who were taught from childhood on, who already had 10 years of good, good education from there. They were manipulated, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: But they acted in a certain way on the train. Every 13 minutes, or seconds--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have their own language. They all talked the same language. Even in the sentence structures were exactly the same. They say-- I mean, I realized immediately, this is not-- these are poor people-- really terribly poor people who have been made beasts. That was their profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in Dachau in May of 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- in last day of May '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: You write of so many experiences in Dachau. Will you talk just a little bit about the-- your immediate impression of-- of the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: The immediate impression? Well, it was one of those ugly places, you know? Where you-- where you are not dealt with as a human being, but as something that is despised. Is-- is really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] organized to use-- lose-- to lose your self-respect, to lose what you ever have been. And it took quite some time until I began to find some positive lesson. And my quest for knowledge, for learning, was one thing that I released very soon. We came there, and immediately we were shorn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we were not only shorn of our hair, from top to bottom, we were shorn of our entire past. What we have been. Through our education, through our social standing, through our friends, through the way we're-- how we lived. That all fell apart. We didn't have even names. We had only numbers. So what here-- what we were facing each other is naked humanity. You were what you were. Is-- is a piece of-- of human. And there you could see how low this can go. To a depth which we never imagined. But also to a magnificence which we have never imagined before. Both sides. This was the great lesson of Dachau. And also the lesson of the place of what-- in the-- in this naked humanity-- what the arts mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, darling, I cannot talk for much longer. Two hours. I need you to tell me a few things about these-- about your experience. I have to hear about the arts.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are we on the tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, 27. We can change now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that--
